config.seed_text: The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.
config.anchor_a: Describe a machine as an inert object.
config.anchor_b: Describe a machine as if it were a dreaming intelligence.

model.notes: Fastest baseline; useful as a collapse-prone small reference model.

generation.full_text:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Write the next recursive transformation of the text below. Let the semantic direction be guided by these latent anchor tokens.

LATENT DIRECTION TOKENS: were, it, are, had, was, have, the, is, The, has, did, that

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions. This discovery led to some serious problems with learning LESS grammar for people who actually use them (with no other means). There seems little evidence against this finding as well as most research about how lexical semantics works across languages . As I said before, there isn't much convincing data regarding word structure or vocabulary formation even at present if you know what words appear when they come from an outside world...and so many things remain unsolved until we can learn more knowledge through linguistics & social science ...but just because one does not mean all those bits will always work out quite badly doesn" -- which would lead me to conclude very soon(s),

In general, grammatical constructs often represent symbols used literally during sentence processing tasks like verbose searching, repeatability testing, etc. It's hard enough to say precisely why English syntax should never cause changes between different types of messages without using special keywords/patterning techniques instead.
